noble: The fourth child is not entitled to any inheritance
farmer: The land became barren and foul
exile: Grew up without a name
criminal: Grew up on the streets
monastic: Tithed to a religious order
military: On leave to find your fortune
scholar: Expelled from the academy for an unsanctioned theory
wilds: Born in a frontier settlement that no longer exists
labourer: Worked a trade since before you could read
merchant: Apprenticed to a caravan master who vanished

noble: Last in a long line
farmer: Tended to the living trees
exile: Child of a lost kingdom
criminal: Violater of ancient laws
monastic: First time away from the sacred grove
military: Former guard of an assassinated Elf Lord
scholar: Studying mortal magic in secret
wilds: Wandered too far from the grove and forgot the way back
labourer: Bound to mortal service to repay a debt
merchant: Trades in favors that are never quite what they seem

noble: I must prove my worth to my parents for the throne
farmer: Master of mushrooms and manure
exile: Sworn to never return to the depths
criminal: Escaped slave miner
monastic: Sent as part of a cultural exchange
military: Deserted a clan war you no longer believe in
scholar: Obsessed with a lost theory
wilds: Surface-born and never set foot in the ancestral halls
labourer: Forge-hands since before your beard came in
merchant: Selling family heirlooms to settle a debt of honor

noble: Kicked out after a coup
farmer: Disgraced swineherd
exile: Last of a despised clan
criminal: The charges never stuck long enough to matter
monastic: They still doubt my worth
military: Conscripted as a scout no one expected to survive
scholar: Reads better than anyone gives a goblin credit for
wilds: Raised by a warren that no longer exists
labourer: The dirtiest jobs always fell to you
merchant: Runs a stall everyone swears they never bought from

noble: Sent to fulfil a prophecy
farmer: A simple chosen life interrupted
exile: Cast away from a world that time has forgotten
criminal: Sent to shatter an uneasy balance
monastic: A cult hero seeking escape
military: Conscripted into a war that isn't yours
scholar: Cataloguing this world like it's an experiment
wilds: Stranded here when the crossing closed behind you
labourer: Working off the cost of a one-way passage
merchant: Trading knowledge of places no one here has seen

noble: Created to replace a lost child
farmer: Built for endless labour
exile: Created with forbidden magic
criminal: Programmed without a moral compass
monastic: Animated by divine mystery
military: Built as a weapon and decommissioned before the war ended
scholar: Reprogrammed yourself to want to learn
wilds: Abandoned in the wilderness when your maker disappeared
labourer: Still doing the job you were built for unpaid
merchant: Appraises goods with unsettling perfect precision

noble: The bastard child of a king and a beast
farmer: More comfortable amongst the animals
exile: Branded an abomination
criminal: An agent of the resistance
monastic: Grew up in the one place you've found acceptance
military: Fought on a front line they wouldn't let you lead
scholar: First of your kind admitted to the university and they never let you forget it
wilds: Raised by the pack
labourer: Hired for the strength and never the skill
merchant: Bartered for safe passage your whole childhood

noble: Sent on behalf of the council
farmer: Sent into the world for finer seeds
exile: A heretic that loves the world of the big people
criminal: Nobody suspects the halfling
monastic: Not even you know how you ended up at the monastery
military: Too small to fight and too stubborn to be left behind
scholar: The council's former record-keeper who knows too much
wilds: Lost the caravan and prefer the quiet
labourer: Worked twice as hard to be taken half as seriously
merchant: You were supposed to take over the business, but it's gone

noble: Preferred the slow knife of rumor and hex
farmer: The blight came; you learned to send it elsewhere
exile: They feared it before you learned to use it
criminal: Hexes leave fewer witnesses than conventional methods
monastic: The sacred texts had an appendix nobody mentioned
military: The general wanted a weapon; you became several
scholar: Applied malediction: the thesis alarmed the faculty
wilds: The old things in the dark taught their own language
labourer: Years of thankless work distilled into something lasting
merchant: Every deal is cursed eventually; you go first

noble: The temple needed a patron; you needed a purpose
farmer: Prayed for the harvest and something answered
exile: Healing others was the only thing that felt like home
criminal: Saw too many people die badly; decided to stop it
monastic: The order chose you before you could choose anything
military: Kept the soldiers alive long enough to matter
scholar: Studied the theology of miracles until one worked
wilds: The land's suffering needed someone willing to mend it
labourer: The accidents never stopped; you learned to fix people faster
merchant: People trust a healer farther than a trader

noble: Magic was beneath the family; that made it interesting
farmer: The ley lines make more sense when you grew up on them
exile: The only thing they couldn't take was in your head
criminal: Fire solves most problems faster than a crowbar
monastic: Arcane and monastic discipline share more than expected
military: Reassigned from artillery when you became the artillery
scholar: The theory was always right; the scale just needed adjusting
wilds: The magic out there doesn't ask permission and neither do you
labourer: Spent years moving things by hand; found a better way
merchant: A mind built for trade routes adapts well to spell geometry

noble: The estate's garden spoke first; the forest spoke next
farmer: The soil isn't just for growing things; it listens
exile: The wilderness took you in when people wouldn't
criminal: Used nature as cover until it started using you back
monastic: The order's prayers to nature eventually got a response
military: The terrain was always the best weapon; you learned to wake it
scholar: The natural philosophy papers were wrong; you went to check
wilds: Raised in the deep places; still half in them
labourer: Every forest you cleared pushed back; eventually you stopped
merchant: The forest can be a road if you ask it correctly

noble: The estates were beautiful; the hunting grounds were better
farmer: Pest control for the fields grew into something larger
exile: Living outside borders taught you to see them first
criminal: Always operated at range; became very good at it
monastic: The scouting missions never returned; you were the exception
military: Assigned to reconnaissance; never quite returned to formation
scholar: Wrote the definitive survival manual; then field-tested every chapter
wilds: Grew up knowing where everything was by sound alone
labourer: Spent years reading the land until it started reading back
merchant: Supply routes need someone who sees trouble arriving early

noble: Selling your skills felt more honest than inheriting them
farmer: The farm couldn't pay in coin; the contract could
exile: Work without allegiance suits someone with nowhere to return
criminal: The difference between criminal and mercenary is paperwork
monastic: The martial training needed an outlet the order wouldn't sanction
military: Left service when the pay stopped matching the risk
scholar: Studied the economics of force; became a practitioner
wilds: Protection work suits someone who knows how to vanish
labourer: The body is a tool; might as well rent it well
merchant: The caravan needed a guard more than you needed the caravan

noble: The title came with armor; you earned what goes inside
farmer: Swore to protect the harvest from men on horses; became one
exile: Honor survived when everything else was stripped away
criminal: Took the king's pardon in exchange for shield duty; kept both
monastic: The order needed a sword arm; you were volunteered
military: Rose through the ranks by not dying when others did
scholar: Studied defensive formation theory until you were the one teaching it
wilds: Forged your own armor because no armorer came that far
labourer: Built walls for others your whole life; learned to be one
merchant: The caravans needed protection and a reputation; you offered both

noble: The commission bought a rank; the battlefield built the rest
farmer: Conscripted; discovered an unexpected talent for it
exile: Fighting for someone else's cause is still fighting
criminal: Took the enlistment over the sentence; some habits carried over
monastic: The order trained for war; peace never quite settled afterward
military: Never left; never had reason to
scholar: Wrote the manual on unit tactics; then went to test it
wilds: Survival out there taught formation before anyone used the word
labourer: Joined up because it paid the same and the work mattered more
merchant: Every supply line needs someone who can defend it

noble: Renounced everything; kept the discipline
farmer: The body built by labor turned out to be the weapon
exile: Stillness came easier with nothing left to protect
criminal: Found that unarmed was still very very armed
monastic: Born to this; shaped by every blow received and returned
military: Left service looking for something worth the pain
scholar: Mapped the body's pressure points with academic precision
wilds: The wilderness has no weapons; you became the answer
labourer: Years of physical work became something more deliberate
merchant: Bodyguard work was the practice space; you outgrew the clients

noble: Dueling was the one family tradition worth keeping
farmer: Too fast for the fields; found a better use for it
exile: A blade and quick feet carry farther than a home
criminal: The most dangerous person in the room doesn't need a plan
monastic: The order practiced forms; you practiced them faster than they liked
military: Transferred out of formation; worked better alone anyway
scholar: Applied geometry to swordplay; wrote a pamphlet on it
wilds: Learned to move without sound; then without restraint
labourer: The dock fights taught footwork no academy ever would
merchant: The blade opened doors the coin couldn't

noble: The servants saw everything; you learned from them
farmer: Moving quietly was survival long before it became a profession
exile: Disappearing is a skill best learned when someone is looking
criminal: The craft was never about the money; you loved the work
monastic: The vow of silence had applications the order hadn't considered
military: Assigned to infiltration; never fully came back out
scholar: Researched the underworld academically until they recruited you
wilds: Prey learns to be invisible or it becomes food
labourer: Night shifts taught you that most doors open if you know how
merchant: Supply chain management is logistics; this is more interesting

noble: Performance was always expected; you made it mean something
farmer: The work songs carried weight you didn't understand until you left
exile: All you took when they sent you away was the music
criminal: The best con ends with the audience applauding
monastic: The chants worked; you needed to know exactly why
military: The soldiers needed morale; you supplied it with interest
scholar: Proved that music affects the nervous system — the hard way
wilds: The land has songs no concert hall has ever heard
labourer: Sang to make the work bearable; the song did more than that
merchant: Every deal is a performance; you became expert at both

noble: The title burned down; only the fury survived
farmer: Every plow breaks eventually; so did you
exile: When everything was taken, force was all that remained
criminal: Started the fight before anyone finished explaining the crime
monastic: Left when they insisted on patience
military: Led from the front until there were no orders left
scholar: Every theory of war ends with the same fist
wilds: The wilderness has no use for those who hesitate
labourer: The body knew what it was for before you did
merchant: Negotiations ended badly; everything after was simpler
